intro: Migrating scripts from JavaScript (Node.js) to Bash switches execution from an asynchronous, event-driven runtime to a native, process-driven shell environment. Developers make this change to minimize dependencies, maximize compatibility across Linux systems, and directly orchestrate operating system processes without the overhead of NPM modules. This transition swaps object-oriented or functional paradigms for text-stream processing and standard I/O pipes.

diffs.from: Dynamic type system resolving to primitives (strings, numbers, booleans) and memory-allocated objects.
diffs.to: Untyped system where every variable is fundamentally treated as a character string unless context forces integer evaluation.

diffs.from: C-style lexical scoping requiring keywords like const/let, explicit brackets, and object literal notation.
diffs.to: Command-line syntax built on positional parameters, whitespace-sensitive variable evaluation ($VAR), and condition tokens like [ and [[

diffs.from: Virtual machine heap allocation controlled by automated generational garbage collection routines.
diffs.to: Minimal memory management; relies on short-lived process tracking, environment tables, and operating system file descriptors.

diffs.from: Implicit event loop executing non-blocking system tasks via callbacks, promises, and worker threads.
diffs.to: Synchronous sequence execution by default; background processes are spawned explicitly via ampersands (&) and tracked with PIDs.

diffs.from: Extensive API covering cryptographic operations, buffers, network protocols, and third-party modules.
diffs.to: Relyant on standard GNU/Linux command-line utilities (awk, sed, grep, cut) and POSIX-compliant system calls.

examples.title: Directory Iteration and File Checking
examples.description: Checking for the existence of files within a directory layout.
examples.toCode:
DIR="./config"
if [ -d "$DIR" ]; then
  echo "Config directory exists."
fi

examples.title: Executing External Commands and Capturing Output
examples.description: Running an external tool and binding its stdout value to a local script variable.

tips.title: Quote Your Variables Constantly
tips.body: Always wrap string variables in double quotes (e.g., "$VAR") to prevent Bash from performing word splitting and glob expansion on spaces.

tips.title: Strict Error Handling Configuration
tips.body: Bash does not fail on errors by default. Put 'set -euo pipefail' at the top of scripts to exit instantly if any command fails or variable is unassigned.

tips.title: Whitespace Matters Crucially
tips.body: Spaces are delimiters in Bash. Writing 'MYVAR = "val"' will fail because Bash interprets MYVAR as an executable command; use 'MYVAR="val"'.

tips.title: Replace Array Methods with Text Utilities
tips.body: Instead of using .filter() or .map(), pipe textual output directly into Linux streams using tools like grep, awk, and xargs.

faqs.question: How do I handle complex JSON processing inside a Bash script?
faqs.answer: Since Bash lacks native object/JSON structures, developers pipe text payloads into 'jq', a lightweight command-line JSON processor.

faqs.question: What is the equivalent of a Try/Catch block in Bash?
faqs.answer: Bash uses exit status codes (0 for success, 1-255 for errors). You handle exceptions by appending conditional or operators: 'command || handle_error_function'.

faqs.question: Are arrays supported inside Bash?
faqs.answer: Modern Bash (v4+) supports indexed arrays and associative arrays (key-value maps), though their definition and iteration syntax are highly specialized.
